findings.description: Logger uses the hardcoded name 'search' instead of the conventional __name__, hampering per-module log routing and filtering.
Type hints use unparameterized dict (list[dict]) instead of dict[str, Any] or a typed model, weakening static checking.
A new SQLite connection is opened and torn down on every method call with no reuse or pooling, adding per-request overhead.
page and page_size have no lower-bound validation, so page<=0 or zero/negative page_size yields negative offsets and Python slice wraparound producing wrong or empty windows.
page_size above MAX_PAGE_SIZE is silently clamped to 100 with no signal to the caller, which can desynchronize client-side pagination math.
search() fetches the entire users table with fetchall() and filters, sorts, and paginates in Python instead of pushing WHERE/ORDER BY/LIMIT/OFFSET into SQL, costing O(N) time and memory per request.
name_pattern is compiled as a user-supplied regex and run against every display_name, enabling ReDoS (catastrophic backtracking) CPU-exhaustion denial of service.
An invalid name_pattern raises an uncaught re.error from re.compile, crashing the call instead of surfacing a validation error.
If display_name is NULL in the database the dict value is None, so matcher.search(None) raises TypeError here and .lower() raises AttributeError in the sort at line 62.

Off-by-one pagination: with the documented 1-based page the offset must be (page-1)*page_size, but page*page_size makes page=1 skip the first page_size users so the first page of results is unreachable.
Public methods by_email, departments, and deactivate lack docstrings describing parameters and return semantics.
Email comparison uses case-sensitive equality (SQLite default), so by_email misses case-variant addresses unless the column is declared COLLATE NOCASE.
Rows with NULL department produce None entries in the returned list, violating the declared list[str] contract.
deactivate performs a destructive state change keyed only by user_id with no authorization check at this layer, an IDOR risk if exposed directly to untrusted callers.
The UPDATE lacks an AND deleted = 0 guard, so deactivating an already-deactivated user still matches a row and returns True.
